Justification: The study used observed time series of species level catch data from a stationary fish trap and analysed log transformed CPUE dynamics over 25 years. Resilience was defined as return time or return rate to equilibrium from an ecological disturbance or displacement from equilibrium and quantified as the negative of the leading eigenvalue of the fitted MAR(1) community matrix. The paper explicitly framed community stability as being revealed by three indices resistance resilience and reactivity. The target variable used in the MAR(1) model was CPUE in kg which is a catch biomass index aggregated into three trophic groups. The analysis is based on a trap anchored 200 m from the Camogli coast of the Ligurian Sea and Figure 1 describes this location off the coast of Camogli Italy. Predator depletion is discussed as occurring over a period of strong long term predator decline and fishing effort was identified as the most important predictor of declining community stability which supports an unmanipulated biological resource use disturbance treated as ongoing. The fishing pressure increased over the study period and there is no defined end to the driver within the observation window which matches a press disturbance. The data span 1950 to 1974 which is 25 years giving an observation duration of 25 times 365 days.

The study quantifies vegetation resistance and recovery using MODIS-derived NDVI indices described as indices for vegetation resistance and recovery. Resistance is defined as Rs = NDVI 2011 / NDVI 2010 and recovery is defined as Rc = NDVI 2012 or 2013 or 2014 / NDVI 2011. These indices use observed remote sensing time series across pre-drought drought and post-drought years so the measurement is observed. The introduction and methods present resilience as vegetation resilience namely resistance and recovery. Resistance is quantified as the ratio of NDVI during drought relative to pre-drought which represents capacity to maintain greenness during stress. Recovery is quantified as the ratio of post-drought NDVI to pre-drought or drought year NDVI which captures ability to recover after drought stress and is interpreted as rebound relative to 2011 status. The target variable used to compute both indices is NDVI which the paper uses to represent vegetation health and greenness and thus functions as a vegetation index proxy for ecosystem productivity. The study area is the Lower Mississippi River Basin spanning Missouri Illinois Kentucky Tennessee Arkansas Mississippi and Louisiana and the drought events are naturally occurring. The disturbance is drought severity from the U.S. Drought Monitor which is a climatic stressor and is analyzed as a continuous time window to capture cumulative and compounding effects. The observation window is from March 2 2010 to June 25 2013 and NDVI is extracted for April to October for 2010 through 2014 so the duration between first and last measurement is 1576 days. The first author affiliation is Appalachian State University in the United States. The riparian systems include floodplains wetlands and bottomland hardwood forests and wetlands are explicitly listed as a riparian system type in the study area description. The focal subject is riparian vegetation and plant communities.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + stress
Target variable type: Land use; Regional network parameter; Socio-eco parameter
Justification: The paper develops a regional ecosystem health assessment framework described as a Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Stress framework and gives the formula EHI = EVI×EOI×ERI×ESI. Ecosystem resilience is not measured as recovery dynamics but is computed as ERI = sum Ai×RCi where RCi is an ecosystem resilience coefficient assigned by land use type and taken from previous research with coefficients calculated by a weighted average of stability and resilience. Ecosystem organization is quantified using landscape pattern indices from land use data including SHDI SHEI COHESION CONTAG DIVISION MNPARA and AWMFDI. Ecosystem stress is quantified from land use intensity soil erosion intensity and rocky desertification intensity and is treated as external pressure. Because resilience is derived from static land use proportions and assigned coefficients rather than observed recovery after disturbance the resilience measurement is inferred. The study uses RS and GIS datasets including MODIS NDVI and land use and socio-economic data for Guizhou Province China. The study area is given as Guizhou Province 24•37′-29•13′N and 103•36′-109•35′E and the affiliated institution is Guizhou University in Guiyang China. The habitat context includes multiple land use types explicitly listed in the resilience coefficient table including cropland forestland grassland water construction land and bare land.

The study states that interaction network resilience was calculated using the network resilience parameter βeff and that this parameter is quantifiable from an ecological network and defines the state of an ecosystem against disturbances such as loss in interactions species interaction weight or combinations therefrom. The methods estimate βeffplants and βeffbees from quantitative plant bee interaction network data pooled across four sampling sessions per plot rather than measuring recovery dynamics of a state variable after a defined disturbance. The analyses assess changes in βeff along an elevation gradient and quantify effects of climate diversity network structure and landscape configuration on βeff using GAM multimodel inference and SEM. Because resilience is evaluated through a network proxy parameter βeff rather than direct observation of recovery time recovery rate recovery degree resistance latitude or invariability of the system after disturbance the measurement type is inferred. No explicit equation style framework is provided that defines resilience as a sum of components or embeds resilience within a higher level variable. The target variable used to quantify resilience is the interaction network property βeff derived from plant bee interaction matrices so the target variable type is interaction. The resilience is quantified for plant and bee interaction networks which are multi species assemblages so the level is community. Data were collected by field observations of plant bee visitation frequencies across 50 plots in Kenya between July 2019 and April 2020. The first author affiliation list includes International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology in Nairobi Kenya so the institution country is Kenya. The study area coordinates are given for Taita Hills and Murang’a County Kenya and the average of the provided coordinate ranges is approximately S1.09 E37.36 with a maximum deviation sum of about 3.1 degrees. The plots include savannah shrubland forest tree cover grassland cropland and built up areas and are described as an intermix of natural and anthropogenic habitats so the habitat type is multi. Disturbance is discussed conceptually but no disturbance event is imposed or analysed as a pulse or press for quantifying βeff so disturbance fields and observation duration for disturbance response are NA.

The study applied controlled trampling treatments because it states that experimental trampling was conducted and that lanes received 0 to 500 passes with one lane as a control. Vegetation response was measured using observational data because it estimated the cover of each vascular plant species and of mosses and lichens and calculated relative vegetation cover. Resilience is defined as recovery because it states that resilience refers to the ability of a vegetation type to recover following the cessation of trampling. The resilience index is based on change in relative vegetation cover during the year after trampling expressed as a fraction of the change in cover that occurred 2 weeks after trampling. This quantification is a recovery degree because it is an amount of increase in cover over the year relative to the initial loss rather than a time to recovery. The target variable used to quantify resilience is cover because the resilience index is computed from relative vegetation cover. The level is community because the primary response variable is total cover summed across species for each vegetation type and indices were calculated for each vegetation type. The approach is field experiment because trampling treatments were administered in early summer at 18 experimental study sites in five mountainous regions. The institution country is the United States because the first author affiliation is Aldo Leopold Wilderness Research Institute Forest Service US Department of Agriculture in Missoula Montana USA. The field country is the United States because sites were established in mountain regions in the United States and Table 1 lists states Washington Montana Colorado New Hampshire and North Carolina. The habitat type is multi because the study includes 18 vegetation types across multiple regions and includes forested and non forested types with overstorey canopy cover ranging from 0 to 95 percent. The taxon is Plantae because the measured subjects are vascular plant species and vegetation types. The disturbance is manipulated structural because trampling passes are an imposed physical disturbance that changes vegetation cover and structure. The disturbance pattern is pulse because trampling was administered as an event and recovery was measured 2 weeks and 1 year after trampling. Observation duration is 365 days because follow up measurements were taken 1 year after trampling.

The study states it uses the Vigor-Organization-Resilience model and defines ecosystem health using vigor organization and resilience. Resilience is not measured from recovery dynamics but is calculated as ER = sum of Ai times RCi where RCi is an ecosystem elasticity coefficient derived by combining expert scores and previous studies and Ai is land use area. This makes resilience an indicator constructed from land use areas and expert derived coefficients rather than observed recovery after disturbance. The methods describe land use data POI data and remote sensing imagery and the resilience indicator is based on land use type areas. The study area is Jiaozhou Bay in Qingdao Shandong Province China and the coastal zone is defined as townships within 10 km of the coastline. The first author affiliation is Ocean University of China in Qingdao China. The location map and text identify Jiaozhou Bay and Qingdao and the coordinates are not provided so the response uses an approximate central coordinate for Jiaozhou Bay. The analysis period is 2012 to 2022 but because measurement is inferred the disturbance pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable.

The study defines resilience as a function of resistance and recovery stating that resilience can be considered as a function of resistance and recovery. Disturbance impact is quantified as the log response ratio of pre to post disturbance percentage coral cover and recovery is analysed from data points following the disturbance. Recovery rate is quantified by fitting linear models to coral cover versus years since disturbance in an approximate 5 year time window and extracting the slope as annual recovery rate in percent coral cover per year. The target variable used in both impact and recovery analyses is total hard coral cover before and after disturbance. The major taxa studied are scleractinian hard corals. The study compiles observational literature data from tropical coral reefs globally and the location is stated as tropical coral reefs globally. Disturbances are acute storms and temperature stress and coastal run off from flooding events which are treated as short lived events linked to climate. The recovery window is explicitly a 5 year period following disturbance which corresponds to 1825 days. The first author affiliation is The University of Western Australia and CSIRO and Australian Institute of Marine Science in Australia. The habitat is coral reefs in shallow coastal and shelf waters consistent with marine neritic environments. Disturbance types include climatic events such as storms and marine heatwaves and hydrological flooding events producing run off.

The study states that directly observing autotrophic biomass is difficult and that mechanistic models fit to productivity time series can infer underlying biomass dynamics. It describes a latent biomass time series model that estimates daily growth and disturbance of autotrophic biomass as a latent state and fits it to daily gross primary production time series from six rivers. It explicitly defines resilience as fast recovery by stating relatively high ecosystem resilience i.e. fast recovery in algal biomass and primary productivity. It also states that it estimates maximum autotrophic biomass growth rates rmax as a proxy for the resilience of primary producers to flood disturbances and later calls rmax a proxy for ecosystem productivity resilience. Because resilience is treated as a proxy derived from a latent state rather than directly measured recovery of biomass itself the measurement is inferred. The quantification is inferred because resilience is quantified via rmax and daily maximum percent increase in biomass calculated as (ermax−1)×100. The target variable used to infer resilience is ecosystem productivity time series specifically daily gross primary production. The modelling is implemented using Bayesian inference via Hamiltonian Monte Carlo in Stan and uses out of sample prediction tests. The first author affiliation is University of Nevada Reno in the United States. The rivers are identified with US state abbreviations and USGS discharge data are used indicating the field data are from the United States. The text does not provide coordinates for the six sites. The system is rivers and streams which fit the wetland habitat category. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type pattern and observation duration are coded as NA per instructions.

The study used outdoor experimental stream channels and imposed simulated spates by increasing current to about 60 cm per second and scouring tiles with a high pressure hose. It defined resistance as change in algal biomass and taxonomic structure across a disturbance and it assessed resilience as convergence with a temporally changing undisturbed control community. Resilience was assessed in terms of biomass parameters specifically cell numbers and community composition. Resilience quantification used rates of cell accrual after spates with net algal growth rates estimated from the rate of change in natural log transformed cell densities during each post spate recovery period. Community composition resilience was evaluated using trajectories on detrended correspondence analysis ordination plots. The target variables directly used for resilience were cell densities of diatoms and zygnematalean green algae and diatom assemblage composition. The work was a field experiment because it was conducted in outdoor experimental stream channels at the University of Louisville experimental stream facility drawing water from Harts Run. The first author affiliation line gives University of Louisville in the United States. The field site is Bernheim Forest Nature Preserve in Bullitt County Kentucky USA and the water source is Harts Run draining a basin of mixed hardwood forest. The disturbance is a pulse because each spate lasted 2 hours and sampling occurred immediately after each spate and at subsequent 3 day intervals. The observation window for disturbance and resilience spans from day 0 to day 33 as shown in the sampling schedule and described as 33 days following a simulated spate.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability of a system to absorb changes to maintain the same identity and states that functional evenness has been used as a proxy for the resilience of communities. The study quantifies functional evenness and other diversity metrics and uses them to compare invaded and non-invaded communities and to relate diversity to human disturbance and landscape heterogeneity. The discussion states that non-invaded showed higher resilience i.e. FEve than invaded communities and that invaded community diversity has a lower resilience to disturbance. Because resilience is treated as a proxy based on diversity metrics rather than measured as recovery dynamics after a disturbance it is inferred. The paper discusses resilience alongside resistance and also links higher diversity to resistance to invasion without defining a single higher level variable. Bird communities were surveyed with point counts in the Tagus estuary area Portugal indicating field observation. The first author affiliations are at the University of Lisbon and CIBIO InBIO in Portugal. The study area is the Tagus estuary area around Lisbon so an approximate coordinate is provided and the sampling points are within one region so the range is coded as 0. The landscape includes forest agriculture urban shrubland and wetland so the habitat type is multi. The focal organisms are birds so the taxon is Animalia. Disturbance is discussed as human disturbance but resilience measurement is inferred so disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable.

Target variable type: Land use; Regional network parameter; Ecosystem function; Abiotic parameter; Socio-eco parameter
Justification: The paper states it developed an ecological resilience assessment process chain across the disturbance carrying recovery dimensions and names it the D-C-R three-dimensional process model chain with ecological disturbance force ecological carrying capacity and ecological recovery capacity. Ecological resilience is quantified as an index ERI computed from EDF ECC and ERC with assigned weights and with EDF described as inverse normalized and ECC and ERC as normalized positive values. The indicators used to build EDF ECC and ERC include land use vulnerability distance from water bodies population density nighttime lights GDP road network density landscape metrics from Fragstats ecosystem service outputs from the InVEST model and recovery indicators including NDVI and NPP and land use recovery and sewage treatment and education and health institutions. The methods describe weighted calculations on these indicators and exploratory spatial data analysis and Geodetector to analyze spatiotemporal variations from 2000 to 2020 rather than measuring recovery dynamics of a specific disturbed system state variable after a defined disturbance event. The study area is the Dongting Lake region spanning Hunan and Hubei provinces and the case study cities are Yueyang Changde Yiyang and Jingzhou. The first author affiliation is Central South University in Changsha China. The study uses remote sensing imagery and other spatial datasets standardized to 1 km by 1 km and mapped in ArcGIS which is field observation and it also uses models including InVEST and Geodetector and weighting methods which are modeling and simulation. No explicit disturbance event response is quantified and the disturbance force dimension represents likelihood and intensity of potential disturbances so disturbance type pattern and observation duration for recovery after disturbance are not specified. The land use map and description indicate multiple habitat types including lake waters floodplains hills and low mountains and peripheral forest areas so the dominant habitat type cannot be reduced to a single IUCN class.

The study states that it assessed ecosystem stability using NDVI from 2001 to 2022 and ecosystem responses to changes in precipitation soil moisture and potential evapotranspiration using an ARx model. It defines resistance and resilience as two aspects of ecosystem stability and says resilience characterizes the ability to recover its original state after disturbances. In the ARx model NDVI anomaly at time t is modeled with NDVI anomaly at time t minus 1 and hydroclimatic anomaly terms and the coefficient alpha characterizes resilience. The text explains that a larger absolute alpha implies a slow rate of returning to an equilibrium state from a disturbance which is a recovery rate concept. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is NDVI anomaly and NDVI is used as an indicator to reflect changes in ecosystem status which is a vegetation index used as an ecosystem characteristic indicator. The approach is remote sensing observation processed in Google Earth Engine rather than a manipulative experiment. The first author affiliation is Lanzhou University in China. The study area is the arid region of Northwest China with coordinates 73E to 107E and 34N to 49N and the average coordinate is reported. The disturbance is hydroclimatic anomalies including precipitation anomalies and soil moisture anomalies and potential evapotranspiration anomalies treated as external disturbances and exposure is defined by frequency of extreme events over 2001 to 2022 growing seasons indicating a sustained anomaly regime. The observation window spans 2001 to 2022 with MODIS 16 day data aggregated to monthly giving 22 years or 8030 days.

The introduction defines the study focus as engineering resilience meaning the return time of individual plant species in a community to the equilibrium after abrupt change events. The methods impose a one year change event in groundwater level and salinity in process based model simulation experiments with a 50 year burn in and 100 simulation years after return to previous conditions. Resilience is aggregated into return time and amplitude of the response peak and return time is quantified as the number of years for which control and treatment runs differed significantly after the change event. The disturbance is therefore model imposed and consists of abrupt temporary groundwater level change and salinity change. The disturbance is treated as a discrete one year event so it is a pulse. The process based model response variable used for resilience quantification is species specific relative frequency defined as the proportion of replicate runs in which a species was present. The first author affiliation is Technische Universitaet Braunschweig in Germany. Field data used to estimate SDMs and parameterize the PBM were collected on 318 sites along the German Danish and Dutch Baltic and North Sea coast. The illustrative study site Michaelsdorf is given as 12.56 E and 54.36 N and the broader COMTESS sampling extent is 3.7 to 13.4 E and 51.3 to 56.3 N so the average coordinate is approximately 53.8 N and 8.9 E with a farthest deviation sum of about 4.3 degrees. The habitat described for the study site is mainly grazed grassland and the focal subjects are coastal plant species including Lolium perenne and Scirpus maritimus.

The introduction defines resilience as rate of recovery by stating greater resilience rate of recovery of more species rich assemblages. The methods describe a 27 month field experiment in cleared patches that mimic naturally forming gaps and this is a direct imposed disturbance with subsequent recovery measurements. Recovery rates were quantified by comparing the slope of perennial algal cover over time among treatments and by testing time by treatment interactions on the slope of cover over time. The target variable used for the recovery rate analysis was perennial algal cover because the methods state they analyzed perennial cover to assess recovery rates. The study system is a rocky intertidal community at the Bodega Marine Reserve on the northern coast of California USA with coordinates given as 3881901200N 123 8402400W. The first author affiliation is Center for Population Biology University of California Davis California USA. The disturbance is the experimental clearing by scraping the rock surface within patches bare of all visible algae which is a structural manipulation. The disturbance is treated as a discrete event at the start of the experiment with recovery tracked monthly which matches a pulse disturbance. Observation duration is 27 months from May 2008 to 27 months later when the experiment was terminated and algae were destructively sampled.

The introduction frames resilience as population responses to recurrent disturbances and the methods quantify population characteristics using integral projection models parameterized with annual surveys of 3171 tagged coral colonies between 2016 and 2019. The study states that short-term potential measures describe populations following displacement from stationary equilibrium due to disturbances and defines the damping ratio as demographic recovery describing the rate at which a perturbed population converges back to its asymptotic growth trajectory. This makes the resilience measurement observed because it is quantified from system state variables derived from repeated observations of survival growth fragmentation and recruitment. The quantification aligns with recovery rate because damping ratio is explicitly a rate of convergence back to the asymptotic trajectory. The target variable type is demography parameter because resilience related metrics are derived from survival transitions fecundity recruitment and population growth dynamics. The level is population because the IPMs quantify population growth and recovery metrics for assemblages. The approach combines field observation for the annual tagged colony surveys and modeling and simulation for the IPM based quantification. The first author affiliation is University of St Andrews in the United Kingdom. Field data were collected in eastern Australia and southern Japan. The methods provide GPS coordinates for four regions and the average of those coordinates is approximately S30.3 E153.1 with the farthest point differing by about 57 degrees when summing maximum latitude and longitude differences. The habitat is coral reef communities in nearshore shallow marine settings so Marine.Neritic. The subjects are corals which are animals. Disturbance is treated as unmanipulated climatic variability via SST regimes and as unmanipulated structural displacement from equilibrium due to disturbances without experimental manipulation and the disturbance is considered as recurrent variability over long time series and annual surveys which is press. The observation window for the demographic surveys spans 2016 to 2019 which is about 1095 days.

The methods state that they aimed "to approximate the resilience of each lineage in P. farinosa to climate change" by modeling "the current and future distribution of suitable habitats expressed as the number of climatically suitable cells" using species distribution models and future climate projections. This is not a direct measurement of system recovery dynamics and instead uses modeled habitat suitability as a proxy for resilience so the measurement is inferred. The study also quantifies intraspecific genomic diversity using "expected heterozygosity" and "expected allelic richness" and treats climatic niche breadth as "a proxy for the ecological diversity of each lineage" which are proxy indicators linked to resilience in the introduction and discussion. No explicit equation framework defining resilience is provided and resilience is discussed conceptually alongside genetic diversity and niche breadth. The first author affiliation is "Univ. of Zurich Zurich Switzerland" so the institution country is CH. Occurrence and sampling span the CW Alps W Carpathians Scandinavia and Pyrenees and the introduction lists regions including "Carpathians Alps Iberia" and "British Isles Baltic region and southern Scandinavia" so multiple European countries are involved but coordinates are not provided. The habitat description includes "calcareous fens edges of streams or lakes" indicating Wetland as the major habitat type. The focal organism is the plant "Primula farinosa" so the taxon is Plantae.

Framework: resilience = capacity to remain within a stability domain or state
Justification: Resilience is explicitly defined as the capacity of a system to remain within a stability domain or state and the size of the region between the desired stable state and the separatrix represents the resilience of the stable state. The Methods quantify resilience as the size of the area between the lower stable equilibrium and the unstable equilibrium and also describe flip up and flip down points and critical P input rates for transitions between low and high chlorophyll. This corresponds to a threshold based ability to avoid shifting to an alternative stable state which matches latitude. The response variable used in the model is areal chlorophyll and the paper states chlorophyll levels are affected by phosphorus loading DOC and food web effects and uses areal chlorophyll as the metric managers are interested in controlling visible algal blooms. The study uses a nonlinear deterministic model and stochastic simulations that randomly select driver values and calculate distributions of equilibrium chlorophyll and resilience across 1000 trials and also simulates 150000 lakes for comparative study emulation which is modeling and simulation. Parameters were estimated for northern Wisconsin lakes and the first author affiliation listed is Center for Limnology University of Wisconsin Madison Wisconsin USA. Driver data include a regression for thermocline depth for small lakes in Ontario Canada and harvest data for 210 northern Wisconsin lakes from the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources and DOC data sets for northern Wisconsin and experimental lakes in northern Michigan. Disturbance drivers are imposed in the model as changes in P loading harvest and DOC which represent resource input changes food web alteration by angling and chemical environment changes via dissolved organic carbon. The disturbance is treated as sustained driver levels in equilibrium and Monte Carlo sampling rather than discrete events so it is press. No explicit coordinates are provided for the lake district or the Ontario regression lakes so location and range are NA. The system is lakes and lake districts and the closest IUCN category provided is wetland. The resilience target is an ecosystem state variable rather than a specific taxon so taxon is NA.

The study defines high environmental quality urban areas partly by avian communities with high potential resilience to face ecological stress and it estimates potential resilience by combining species richness and functional redundancy of functional traits. The methods state that potential resilience of bird assemblages was estimated by combining the species richness and redundancy of functional traits and that functional redundancy was calculated using the functional evenness FEve index based on 73 traits plus body mass. Resilience is therefore not measured as recovery after a disturbance but is constructed from proxy indicators species richness and FEve and then discretized into terciles and combined into a categorical potential community resilience. The introduction defines ecological resilience as capacity to absorb environmental changes without altering its main functions but the analysis does not quantify absorption or recovery dynamics. The framework is not expressed as an equation and resilience is discussed conceptually rather than as a component in an explicit formula. The data come from standardized point counts in 15 cities in 10 European countries during the 2018 breeding season which is field observation. The first author affiliation is Czech University of Life Sciences Prague in the Czech Republic. The field countries are the countries listed for the surveyed cities in Table 1. The habitat is urban because point counts were positioned in a gradient of urbanized areas and environments were classified as urban by built up area building density and human density criteria. The taxon is Animalia because the focal subjects are birds and the resilience proxy is based on bird community metrics. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable because measurement is inferred and no disturbance response window is quantified.

The paper proposes a quantitative measure called the Persistence Index to assess the capacity of communities to maintain their functions and services after disturbances and it is formulated from response trait richness and response trait abundance and redundancy rather than from observed recovery dynamics. The index inputs are a species specific response trait matrix and a vector of species relative abundance and the case study uses national forest inventory plot data and a response trait database. Resilience is defined as the ability to undergo disturbance without shifting to an alternative state and resistance is treated as a complementary concept and the study discusses resilience and resistance together without defining a higher level variable that includes resilience. Because resilience is assessed via response traits and their abundance and redundancy rather than time series of ecosystem state recovery the measurement is inferred and quantification is inferred. The traits are response traits and redundancy across species which is an interaction network related proxy. The target community is tree communities in forest stands so the level is community and the taxon is Plantae. The study uses Monte Carlo simulations and also field observation data from the Third Spanish National Forest Inventory. The first author affiliation is in Solsona Spain. The field data are from Peninsular Spain and the Balearic Islands and the map indicates the study area in Spain so an approximate central coordinate is provided with a small range. The habitat is forest because the case study is Iberian forests and forest stands.

Framework: resilience = distance to the boundary of the attraction basin
Justification: The paper states that it mathematically defines resilience as the distance to the boundary of the attraction basin and calls this the distance to the tipping point. It develops a one dimensional function and a scaling factor to compare distances to tipping points across systems. It uses species abundance data and mutualistic network topology from 54 real world mutualistic networks and estimates parameters then uses simulations to obtain recovery rates and distances to tipping points. The resilience quantification is therefore the distance to tipping point which is a threshold boundary concept. The target variable used in the core resilience comparison is abundance including effective abundance and mean field abundance x and proportional abundances yi. The study subjects are plants and animals but the abundance data used for parameter estimation are stated as abundance data for plant species. Perturbations are imposed in simulations by removing nodes and links and changing weights and by state perturbations which are treated as discrete perturbation events in the simulations. The first author affiliation is Shanghai Jiao Tong University in China. The abundance data are from seven papers and include locations explicitly named as Nahuel Huapi National Park in Argentina and Morne Seychellois National Park Mahé in Seychelles and the figure text names Hickling and references include Chile and Tenerife Canary Islands Spain and UK based studies. The text does not provide explicit coordinates in the provided excerpt because Extended Data Fig 1 is referenced for LAT and LONG but the values are not shown here. The time axis for recovery after perturbation is shown with t from 0 to 24 in the figure describing deviation over time.

The abstract states ecological threshold is used to facilitate evaluation of ecosystem resilience and the study derives sea level rise thresholds for salt marsh stability. Methods state spatial patterns of salt marshes were quantified by 26 landscape metrics predicted from a mechanistic wetland change model and thresholds were derived from sigmoid fits and from the first principal component. The study evaluates resilience via thresholds based on landscape metrics and principal components rather than measuring recovery dynamics of a state variable through observed time series. The introduction and discussion repeatedly frame the work as deriving sea level rise thresholds and interpreting larger thresholds as higher resilience such as larger sea level rise thresholds mean higher resilience. The approach is modeling because a mechanistic model predicts salt marsh change under sea level rise scenarios and a neutral model simulates random change. The first author affiliation is The University of Southern Mississippi in the United States. The study area is Grand Bay National Estuarine Research Reserve in Mississippi United States with coordinates 30.37 N 88.43 W. The habitat is salt marsh coastal wetland. Because the resilience quantification is landscape metrics and principal component scores the level is landscape and the target variable type is regional network parameter. Disturbance fields are NA because the measurement is inferred per the instructions.

The abstract states that a Drought Vulnerability Index was constructed integrating exposure sensitivity and resilience as three core dimensions defined in the IPCC framework which defines the framework as vulnerability = exposure + sensitivity + resilience. The Methods state that sensitivity and resilience were derived from autoregressive modeling of vegetation dynamics using vegetation and climate data from 2001 to 2023 and that δ serves as an indirect measure of ecosystem resilience and RI is expressed as 1 – δ which indicates resilience is not measured as an observed recovery trajectory but inferred from an autoregressive coefficient. NDVI from MODIS was used as the vegetation condition variable and the model is NDVIt = α×PDSIt + β×Tt + δ×NDVIt−1 + ε with RI = 1 − δ so the target variable underlying resilience quantification is NDVI as a vegetation index proxy of productivity which fits Ecosystem function. The study uses multi source remote sensing and reanalysis data and monthly PDSI and NDVI time series which is field observation rather than an experiment. The first author affiliation is Nantong University China so the institution country is CN. The study area overview gives the Yangtze River Basin in central China with coordinates 28 N–35 N and 90 E–122 E so the field country is CN and the average coordinate is N31.5 E106.0. Using the given coordinate bounds the farthest point from the average differs by 3.5 degrees latitude and 16 degrees longitude so the sum is 19. The study area includes forests shrublands savannas grasslands wetlands and croplands so the habitat type is Multi. Resilience is quantified for vegetation ecosystems using NDVI so the taxon is Plantae. Because measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration are coded as NA per the instructions.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigor + organization + resilience + services
Justification: The study states it quantitatively assessed ecosystem health based on the Vigor – Organization – Resilience – Services model. Ecosystem resilience is defined as the ability of an ecosystem to recover from external disruptions and is operationalized as a coefficient RCi calculated using NDVI and land use class restoring force coefficients. This uses proxy indicators and assigned coefficients rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a defined disturbance so resilience measurement is inferred. The framework explicitly includes resilience as one component within ecosystem health in the VORS system. Because measurement is inferred quantification is inferred. The resilience related target variables are NDVI and land use class coefficients so the target variable types are physiological indicator and land use. The analysis is at a gridded regional scale across Fujian Province so the level is landscape. Data sources include MODIS NDVI and land cover and other spatial datasets indicating field observation via remote sensing and existing datasets. The first author affiliation includes Qufu Normal University in China so institution country is CN. The study area is Fujian Province in southeastern China so field country is CN. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the province center and the range is set to 0 because only one averaged coordinate is reported. The study includes forestland cropland water and built up land so habitat type is multi. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred and no disturbance response observation window is defined.

The study states that it used a 29-month full-factorial field experiment in a southeastern US saltmarsh and that it measured success by relative growth and the ability to recover from disturbance. Resilience was tested by simulating a physical disturbance through clipping of all above-ground cordgrass biomass and removal of mussels after 16 months. The response variables used for resilience were cordgrass survival and regrowth measured 10 days and 13 months after the simulated disturbance. Survival is described as the percentage of plots still harbouring vegetation after clipping and mussel removal and regrowth is the number of shoots that emerged per transplant after the disturbance. These are direct measurements of system response after an imposed disturbance so the measurement type is observed. The paper does not define resilience within an explicit equation framework and discusses resilience alongside resistance in the discussion without defining a higher-level variable. Quantification includes recovery degree because regrowth and survival after disturbance are compared between treatments at fixed post-disturbance times and includes resistance because the discussion states mussel inclusion enhances the resistance of the transplanted material to future disturbances. The target variables for resilience quantification are survival and shoot emergence which are demography parameters and regrowth in shoots which is biomass related. The focal resilience response is for cordgrass transplants so the level is population. The first author affiliation is Radboud University in the Netherlands. The study site is on Sapelo Island Georgia USA with coordinates given as latitude 31.40749167 and longitude −81.28987601. The disturbance is manipulated clipping and mussel removal which is a structural disturbance and it is a pulse event with measurements taken after the event. The disturbance-resilience observation window spans from the disturbance in August 2014 to the last resilience measurement 13 months later which is 395 days.

The study used long term observational and consecutive ANPP data at least 10 years and calculated resistance and resilience from ANPP during non ECE years ECE years and post ECE years. Resilience was defined as the level of productivity relative to its undisturbed state after an ECE and was calculated using Yn Ye and Ye+1 which is a direct post disturbance response measure. Resistance and resilience were presented as two different indicators for ecosystem productivity responding to ECEs. The resilience calculation uses the difference between ANPP in the year following an ECE and the undisturbed state relative to the ECE impact which matches recovery degree rather than a rate or time. The target variable used for resilience quantification was ANPP which the paper treats as community productivity and thus an ecosystem function. The data were from natural grassland sites and the major taxa studied were herbaceous plant. ECEs were extreme drought and extreme wet classified from SPEI and treated as discrete years and the analysis used ECE year and the following year indicating a pulse framing. The first author affiliation listed Lanzhou University Lanzhou China. The location statement reports 22 sites in Asia and 14 sites in North America and includes an example site in Haibei China and North American prairie studies but no coordinates are provided in the text. The time period is 1980s to 2010s which is approximately 30 years.

The study used a long-term vegetation record from the Sonoran Desert with plant species cover measured 26 times between 1953 and 2009 on 68 permanent transects. Resilience was operationalized in Table 1 as the percentage of occasions where a sample did not undergo transition which the table notes indicate resilience. Transitions were defined as reassignment to an alternative community between successive measurements based on changes in species composition using Bray-Curtis dissimilarity and cluster analysis. Because resilience was quantified as remaining unchanged rather than speed or time to return it matches resistance as the ability to deviate less from the original state after disturbance. The target variable used to quantify transitions and resilience was species composition derived from cover of 22 plant species. The disturbance driver analyzed with transitions was precipitation fluctuation with transitions and dissimilarity positively correlated with precipitation and the transects were not directly manipulated. The precipitation influence was treated across the record without a defined recovery interval so it is press. The first author affiliation is Texas A&M University in the United States and the field site is the Santa Rita Experimental Range in Arizona USA near Tucson. The map and text indicate a single study area so an approximate coordinate near SRER is provided with zero range. The habitat is Sonoran Desert and the subjects are perennial plants.

The paper states that it uses an extended Klausmeier reaction diffusion model and that it employs numerical simulations to assess responses under decreasing rainfall a(t) which is climate change. Resilience is treated through model behaviour under changing rainfall including critical values ac,N and the statement that ecological resilience is determined by the difference between the current rainfall value a and the critical value ac,N that is a minus ac,N. This matches a threshold based capacity to withstand change before flipping to another stability region which corresponds to latitude. The disturbance is modelled climatic because climatic change is characterised only by a change in rainfall and scenarios with decreasing rainfall are analysed. The disturbance is press because rainfall is decreased continuously over the simulation rather than a discrete event. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study is mathematical and uses partial differential equations reduced to ordinary differential equations and presents simulations with specified parameter values and time steps. No new data were used and no empirical field site is used for quantifying resilience so field country and location are not available. The first author affiliation is Leiden University in the Netherlands. Observation duration is taken from the slow climate change simulation described as decreasing rainfall from a equals 1 to a equals 0 in 10^6 time steps.

Framework: urban ecosystem health = pressure + vigor + organization + resilience + service
Justification: The paper proposes the Pressure-Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Service modeling framework and states that UEH is the target layer which consists of five criterion layers P V O R S. Resilience is quantified as habitat quality HQ and Table 3 states resilience refers to the ability of an ecosystem to restore its original structure and function after an external disturbance expressed in terms of habitat quality. UEHI is computed by aggregating normalized indicators with weights and the indicators include HI La FVC SHDI CONTAG AI AWMPFD HQ and ESV. The data sources are Landsat images DEM geographic information and socio-economic data and the method includes land use classification and indicator calculation rather than measuring recovery after a defined disturbance. The study areas are Changle in China and Suez in Egypt with coordinates given as 25•40′ N to 26•04′ N and 119•23′ E to 119•59′ E and 29•50′ N to 30•02′ N and 32•26′ E to 32•35′ E. The first author affiliation is Fujian Normal University in Fuzhou China. The approach uses remote sensing and spatial analysis with Landsat images and ArcGIS and Fragstats which are field observation via remote sensing. The study is about urban ecosystem health in coastal cities and discusses build-up land expansion and afforestation within cities so the dominant habitat context is urban. Because the measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience are not applicable under the instructions.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the degree and rate of recovery of a community from a perturbation. The methods state that they experimentally disturbed grasslands and quantified the recovery of the native plant communities resilience and soil nutrient levels following experimental disturbances. The methods specify that they estimated cover and species richness of all plant taxa to assess community responses to disturbance including resilience defined as the rate and degree of native community recovery from disturbance. The methods describe community recovery as the degree to which native cover and richness metrics in disturbed plots matched levels measured in adjacent undisturbed plots in each post treatment year. The results report resilience to disturbance measured as recovery of native cover and richness in unseeded plots in the 2 years following treatment. The disturbance was imposed by digging up and breaking the soil to a depth of approximately 15 cm and removing plant biomass including roots and shoots. Treatments were initiated at the end of the growing season and plots were read for recruitment during peak growing season the year following treatment and cover was also estimated in the second post treatment year for plots initiated in year 1. Study areas are grassland sites with centroids given for Turkey Montana and La Pampa. The first author affiliation is Rocky Mountain Research Station United States Forest Service in Missoula Montana USA.

The paper quantifies stability and resilience using stochastic differential equation models and computed potential and quasi-potential surfaces rather than using field data. It states that the quasi-potential provides a way to quantify stability in stochastic systems and it uses three classic ecological models with continual stochastic perturbations. It explicitly describes Holling resilience as basin height and also describes basin width as a stability metric and labels it as resilience in other definitions. It presents a framework from Harrison 1979 listing resistance resilience persistence and variability as components under environmental stress and discusses resilience as one aspect of stability for some authors. The quantification selected is latitude because the text defines a metric as the minimum distance between an equilibrium and the boundary of its domain of attraction and calls it the width of the basin of attraction which measures the magnitude of perturbation a system can sustain and still be guaranteed to return. The target variables in the core examples are population densities of phytoplankton resources and zooplankton consumers and nutrient concentration and these are abundances. The approach is modeling and simulation because it generates realizations and computes quasi-potentials using numerical algorithms and reports Euler Maruyama integration and ordered upwind methods. The first author affiliation is Case Western Reserve University in the USA. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is based on models and simulations. Disturbance is modeled as continual Gaussian white noise perturbations to inputs or state variables and the paper emphasizes noise occurs continually with low intensity which matches a press pattern. The disturbance type is classified as climatic because it is described as environmental perturbations and stochasticity and includes examples like extreme climactic conditions and because the perturbations are not structural manipulations of populations but stochastic environmental forcing. Observation duration is taken from example 3 which shows a realization over time interval 0 to 50 and the paper uses continuous time with days not specified so it is converted as 50 years to days using 365 giving 18250.

Framework: ecological adaptability cycle = potential + resilience + connectivity
Justification: The methods define resilience as the ability of ecosystem to restore steady state after disturbance and operationalize it as vegetation greening because the pixels with significantly increased NDVI from 2010 to 2020 were used as resilient pixels and the patches composed of these pixels were resilience patches. This uses NDVI change trend as an indicator rather than measuring recovery after a defined disturbance event so resilience is inferred from a proxy. The study states the three-dimensional framework potential resilience connectivity of ecological adaptation theory and uses it to extract ecological sources so the framework is ecological adaptability cycle equals potential plus resilience plus connectivity. Because measurement is inferred quantification is inferred. The resilience indicator is NDVI trend which the paper treats as vegetation cover recovery ability and it is used alongside ecosystem services importance so the target variable type is ecosystem function. The NDVI and ecosystem services are ecosystem level properties rather than individual population or community metrics so the level is ecosystem. The approach is field observation because the study uses observational remote sensing datasets including MODIS NDVI from 2010 to 2020 and other spatial datasets. The first author affiliation is Xi’an University of Science and Technology in China so institution country is CN. The study area is the Yanhe River Basin in China so field country is CN. The study area coordinates are 36.27 to 37.58 N and 108.41 to 110.29 E so the average location is N37.125 E109.583 and the range is 0 because all locations are within two degrees. The paper states grassland is the major land use type and primary ecological sources are predominantly located where grassland was the dominant land use type so habitat type is grassland. The resilience proxy is vegetation NDVI so the taxon is Plantae. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the inferred measurement rule requires NA for disturbance fields and duration.

The study developed and used a model and states that it was used to explore disturbance recovery dynamics and to assess resilience across disturbance regimes. Fire was imposed in the model because fire size and average frequency were external control parameters and fire appears as a Poisson process and all trees within the fire perimeter are killed. Resilience was evaluated by comparing where total density in the mixed forest exceeded that of monospecific forests and by identifying extirpation thresholds across fire size and fire free interval. The Methods define fitness as species density at equilibrium and compute equilibrium density as the annual average tree density after discarding the first 500 years. The extirpation threshold was defined using a density cutoff and described as the fire rotation demarcating regeneration failure or success. This corresponds to a threshold beyond which the system cannot maintain viable populations which matches latitude. The target variable used in the resilience comparison was adult tree density and total tree density at equilibrium. The level is community because densities were tracked for multiple tree species strategies in mixed forest and total density across all three. The approach is modeling and simulation because the model is implemented in C and simulations were run for 1500 years with parameterized fire regimes. The first author affiliation is University of Wisconsin Madison in the United States. The case study region and parameterization were for subalpine forests in Yellowstone National Park USA. No coordinates were provided so the location was approximated from Yellowstone National Park. Fire events are discrete circular fires with a defined perimeter and time between events so the disturbance pattern is pulse. Observation duration is the simulation length of 1500 years converted to days.

Framework: ecosystem health = vigour + organisation + resilience + services
Justification: The study states it used the Vigour Organisation Resilience Services model to analyse changes in ecosystem health and it defines ecosystem health as derived from the normalised geometric mean of V O R and S with EHI = (V×O×R×S)^(1/4). It defines ecosystem resilience as the ability to maintain structural stability under human activities and then assigns resilience values by land use with a table titled resilience based on land use listing fixed values for cropland forest shrubland grassland water bodies bare land and IPS. This means resilience is not measured from recovery dynamics of a state variable but is constructed from categorical land use values so the measurement is inferred. Because measurement is inferred the quantification must be inferred. The proxy directly used for resilience is land use because the resilience index is taken from land use classes. The work combines observed datasets including land use NDVI NPP and climate with scenario simulation using the PLUS model for 2035 so the approach includes field observation and modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is Chengdu University of Technology in China so the institution country is CN. The study area is Chengdu in Sichuan province China so the field country is CN. The study area extent is given as 102 54 to 104 53 E and 30 05 to 31 26 N so the average coordinate is N30.6556 E103.8917 and the location range is the sum of the maximum latitude difference 0.6806 and maximum longitude difference 1.0144. Land use classes include croplands forests shrublands grasslands water bodies impermeable surfaces and bare land so the habitat type is multi. Because the level is landscape taxon is not applicable. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred from land use coefficients rather than observed disturbance response.

The study imposed a severe summer drought using rainout shelters from 9 July to 6 September 2010 and combined it with a removal experiment of subordinate species. Resistance recovery and resilience were estimated using community above ground biomass measurements with biomass harvested in early July and early September 2009 2010 and 2011. Resilience was calculated as the ratio of total biomass after the drought year 2011 to total biomass before the drought year 2009 which is a recovery degree comparison of post disturbance to pre disturbance state. The introduction explicitly states resilience combines resistance and recovery to provide a measure of community stability. The target variable used to quantify resistance recovery and resilience is above ground biomass including regrowth biomass and total biomass. The work is a field experiment in Swiss Jura grassland at La Frétaz western Switzerland with coordinates 6 34 30 E and 46 50 50 N. The first author affiliation is EPFL Lausanne Switzerland. The subjects are vascular plant species in a semi natural grassland community. The drought is treated as a discrete event with a defined start and stop and recovery is assessed afterward. The observation window for resilience spans from the 2009 pre drought year to the 2011 post drought year which is about two years.

The study quantified drought resilience using individual tree ring width series and calculated six resilience indices with formulas including Resistance = RWDr divided by RWpreDr and Resilience = RWpostDr divided by RWpreDr and Recoveryperiod = Yearrecover minus YearDr. The extreme drought year was identified as 2001 based on climatic data characterized by high temperatures low precipitation and low relative humidity and the extreme values of tree ring width indices were observed during the extreme drought in 2001. RWpreDr and RWpostDr were defined as mean tree ring width of four years before and after 2001 and RWDr was tree ring width in 2001 which shows direct response and recovery dynamics. The disturbance was not experimentally imposed and was treated as an extreme drought event in the climate record. The disturbance is a discrete year 2001 with recovery assessed in subsequent years which matches a pulse framing. The observation window for climate growth analyses was defined as 1980 to 2018 which corresponds to the overlapping period of tree ring width chronologies and climate data. Sampling sites were in the central part of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region in northern China and Table1 reports site coordinates DHB 109.38E 40.75N DQS 111.14E 40.91N and LC 112.29E 40.64N so the average location is N40.77 E110.94. The first author affiliation is Northeast Forestry University Harbin China. The sampled system is semi arid natural Chinese pine broadleaf mixed forests and the target species are Pinus tabuliformis Betula platyphylla and Quercus liaotungensis which are plants.

The study states that it aimed to test resilience of the grass layer to extreme conditions and it sampled all grass individuals before the first fire in 2015 and again after seven annual mid dry season fires in 2022. The disturbance was prescribed burning applied every year from 2015 to 2021 and fire was ensured to be the only factor affecting the vegetation. Resilience was evaluated using observed community state variables including grass density and community composition compared between before and after fire surveys using log response ratio effect sizes and ordination based on species abundances. The discussion concludes that after seven annual burns community composition remained unchanged and grass density doubled evidencing resilience to repeated fires which corresponds to limited change in state under disturbance. The target variables directly used in these resilience statements are density per m2 and community composition from species abundances. The work is a field based prescribed fire experiment at Estação Ecológica de Santa Bárbara in São Paulo Brazil with coordinates given as 22 degrees 46 minutes 33 seconds to 22 degrees 50 minutes 30 seconds south and 49 degrees 10 minutes 27 seconds to 49 degrees 15 minutes 36 seconds west. The grass layer subjects are grasses and the paper describes them as plant species. The disturbance is fire and it is manipulated through annual prescribed burns treated as a continuous regime across years rather than a single event. The observation window spans from the 2015 pre fire survey to the 2022 post fire survey which is about seven years or 2555 days.

The abstract states that time series Landsat imagery during 1994–2015 were used to observe forest recovery of Wu-Ling fire scars in Taiwan. The introduction defines engineering resilience as time to return and then states that in this study engineering resilience is defined as tc and stable state is assumed as the level of pre-disturbed or undisturbed reference area. The methods predict time of complete recovery tc by curve fitting forest recovery trajectories of NBR to an exponential decay function and define complete recovery as 95% of NBRα. This makes resilience quantified as recovery time rather than resistance or recovery degree. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is NBR time series which the introduction describes as sensitive to chlorophyll content and water content and the discussion links to canopy moisture and recovery of vegetation which fits an ecosystem function proxy from remote sensing. The study area and fire history specify wildfire events in 1995 2001 and 2002 at Wu-Ling farm in Taiwan so the disturbance is unmanipulated fire treated as discrete events. The Landsat scene table lists dates from 06/11/1994 to 16/11/2015 which is 7670 days between first and last measurement. The first author affiliation lists National Chung Hsing University in Taichung Taiwan. The study area is Wu-Ling farm in central Taiwan and the habitat is described as pine plantation forest with overstory tree species and understory grass. The resilience mapping is across fire scars and uses observational remote sensing and field investigation rather than an imposed experimental manipulation.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity of an ecosystem to resist and recover from stress. The methods describe a 5-year experiment with rainout shelters set up for around 75 days each summer that excluded 100% precipitation and created warmer and drier conditions. The study quantified resilience using a tipping point threshold because it identified an abrupt loss of resilience when the extreme water stress index exceeded 37 mm 10 day−1 and it used segmented regression models with breakpoints to identify tipping points. The resilience loss was characterized by spring plant cover reduction below 50% and decreased ANPP in rainy years and the tipping point analysis was explicitly applied to spring plant cover versus EWS. Plant cover was visually estimated as total plant cover 0% to 100% in each quadrat at the end of spring and this variable was used in the tipping point quantification. The approach is a field experiment because plots in southern France were fenced and rainout shelters were installed in situ. The site coordinates are given as 43°45′50N and 3°04′50E at the INRAE La Fage station on the Larzac limestone plateau in southern France. The first author affiliation list includes CEFE Univ Montpellier in Montpellier France. The study subjects are plant communities dominated by native perennial graminoids forbs and shrubs. The disturbance is manipulated climatic drought because the treatment is increased summer drought imposed by rainout shelters. The disturbance pattern is pulse because shelters were set up for around 75 days from mid June to end of August and then removed. The observation duration is 5 years as stated as a 5-year experiment and the study monitored responses over five successive years.

Quantification: Recovery degree; Recovery time; Recovery rate
Justification: The study used dendrochronology to reconstruct past growth patterns and calculated resilience indices from standardized ring width series for the 2005 and 2012 droughts. The Methods define resistance as sustaining growth during the stress episode and recovery as the ability of growth to recover after disturbance and resilience as the ability to reach pre disturbance growth levels. The study also calculated recovery period as the time in years needed to recover pre drought growth and average recovery rate as the percentage of recovery during the recovery period. The target variable used for resilience quantification was radial growth derived from ring width indices at the individual level. The drought events were identified using SPEI and treated as discrete dry years 2005 and 2012. Sampling occurred in two Mediterranean shrubland sites in northeastern Spain with coordinates given for Alcubierre and Valcuerna. The first author affiliation is Instituto Pirenaico de Ecología in Zaragoza Spain. The observation window for growth analyses and drought resilience indices is stated as 1980 to 2020 which corresponds to 40 years.

The study develops an assessment framework and evaluates tourism socio-ecological system resilience using an index system and an improved TOPSIS model rather than measuring recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable after a disturbance. The framework is explicitly stated as encompassing four key domains resistance recovery adaptation and renewal. The resilience value is computed as a TOPSIS proximity score Ci ranging from 0 to 1 based on 35 indicators listed in Table 1 such as per capita water resources NDVI PM2.5 tourism income and education indicators. The analysis is conducted for 14 prefecture level units in Xinjiang from 2010 to 2020 using multi source statistical and spatial datasets and methods including Gini coefficient kernel density estimation and obstacle degree model. Because resilience is constructed from proxy indicators and not from direct system recovery trajectories following a defined disturbance the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variables used to quantify resilience are socio ecological indicators describing tourism ecological economic social and cultural subsystems rather than organismal measures. The unit of analysis is prefecture level administrative units across Xinjiang which corresponds to a landscape scale assessment. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang Institute of Ecology and Geography Chinese Academy of Sciences in Urumqi China. The study area is Xinjiang China and Fig 3 shows the location of the study area but no coordinates are provided so an approximate central coordinate for Xinjiang is used and the range is set to 0 because only one averaged coordinate is reported. The study context includes deserts mountains lakes and grasslands so the dominant habitat cannot be reduced to a single IUCN type and is coded as multi. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is not quantified from observed disturbance response dynamics.

The study uses data simulated using the aquatic ecosystem model PCLake which allows the generation of critical transitions in response to gradual changes in phosphorus load. It ran 50-year long eutrophication and oligotrophication scenarios and extracted time series of all model state variables and calculated early-warning indicators on the residuals of the seasonally adjusted and detrended time series. The introduction defines critical slowing down as increased recovery time after a minor perturbation and links it to increasing autocorrelation at lag-1 and variance which are the early-warning indicators used. Because resilience is operationalized through early-warning indicators based on autocorrelation and variance in time series this is a direct quantification from system trajectories and fits observed measurement. The paper does not define a formal equation framework that includes resilience and instead discusses early-warning indicators as indicators of ecosystem resilience. The target variables used for resilience quantification include chlorophyll-a transparency Secchi depth and oxygen saturation which are treated as ecosystem and water quality variables and chlorophyll-a is a productivity related variable. The work is a modeling approach using a virtual lake and the subjects are described as a virtual lake with virtual data. The first author affiliation is Netherlands Institute of Ecology in Wageningen The Netherlands. The virtual lake uses default settings reflecting Dutch weather conditions but no field sampling location or coordinates are provided. The disturbance driver is gradual changes in phosphorus load and stochastic temperature variation used as perturbations which correspond to resource and climatic disturbances imposed in the model and applied continuously over the 50-year scenarios. The observation window for the resilience analysis spans the 50-year runs which is 50 times 365 days.

The study states it is a pulse experiment where whole stands were perturbed by harvesting and recovery was monitored. Ground beetle assemblages were sampled 1 2 and 5 years postharvest using pitfall traps and species were identified and analyzed as Bray Curtis dissimilarities of catch rate for 59 species. The methods say significant differences in pairwise comparisons between harvesting treatments and control stands over time were interpreted as a measure of resistance and potential resilience of ground beetle composition to harvesting. The abstract and discussion describe recovery as recovery of assemblages and recovery to initial conditions relative to uncut control stands and report whether assemblages resemble uncut stands within five years. Because resilience is evaluated by how similar harvested assemblages are to uncut controls over time the quantification corresponds to recovery degree rather than a rate or a time to full return. The focal response variable used for this resilience interpretation is beetle assemblage composition not a proxy index. The first author affiliation is Universite du Quebec a Montreal in Canada and the field site is near Peace River Alberta Canada with coordinates given as 5684601300N 118 82202800W. The disturbance is experimentally applied forest harvesting with retention levels which is biological resource use and it is treated as a discrete pulse harvest in winter 1998 to 1999. Sampling spans from 1999 to 2004 which is five years between first and last measurement.

The study states that it analyses grassland stability and resilience over an exceptionally long time series and reports a 35 years survey with continuous annual sampling that can be treated as individual plot time series. Resilience is defined as the ability to preserve community composition and structure in time and the methods quantify temporal turnover and temporal beta diversity components and abundance based dissimilarity and Shannon diversity and Simpson evenness. The methods explicitly say local community resilience is measured as the stability of communities over short time steps of annual measures and that temporal beta diversity can give reliable stability measures as well as a measure for resilience. The introduction specifies effects of pulse disturbances of climatic origin extremely dry or wet years and a press disturbance of habitat degradation at the landscape scale. The methods identify a perturbation period from 1990 to 1996 followed by wet springs and absence of summer drought between 1997 and 2001. The target variables used for resilience quantification are community composition turnover and diversity indices including Sörensen Simpson nestedness Bray Curtis Shannon diversity and evenness. The study subjects are higher plants angiosperms in calcareous grassland permanent quadrats. The site is Thier de Lanaye near Maastricht at the Belgian Dutch border and the weather station Visé is at 5 km and the plots are within the same grassland complex. The first author affiliation is Irstea in Lyon Villeurbanne France. The observation window spans 35 years which corresponds to 12775 days.

The study parameterized multispecies Markov models using transition probabilities derived from repeated censuses of fixed points in the middle intertidal community of Tatoosh Island Washington USA. Resilience was explicitly assessed as return time because the paper states that extinction of several species affects the resilience of the system as assessed by return time and it calculated return time as 1 divided by the subdominant eigenvalue. The resilience quantification is therefore recovery time because return time is the time to return toward steady state after a perturbation. The target variable used in the resilience calculation is community composition because return time is derived from the community transition matrix describing proportions of points in ecological states. The approach includes field observation because data were collected from fixed points on transects and quadrats censused twice annually through May 1998 and it includes modeling and simulation because the analysis used eigenvalue analysis and simulated time varying stochastic models and manipulated matrices to represent extinctions. The disturbance is model structural because the study altered the model by systematically setting to zero each element involving a target species or ecological state to simulate species extinction and other manipulations. The disturbance pattern is pulse because return time is defined as return from a perturbation and the model examined return times from a perturbation rather than a continuous press driver. The observation duration is five years because the analyses used data collected over a 5 yr period through May 1998. The first author affiliation is University of Chicago in the United States. The field site is Tatoosh Island Washington USA which is a coastal rocky intertidal habitat and the study subjects include multiple taxa including mussels barnacles algae and birds so the taxon is multi. No coordinates are provided in the text so the location is given as NA is not allowed here and the best effort coordinate is the approximate position of Tatoosh Island.

The introduction defines resilience as multi dimensional and states that they quantify aspects of resilience resistance recovery and invariability. The methods state that they used two decades 2000 to 2020 of annual above ground net primary productivity data and calculated resistance recovery and long term invariability from ANPP. Resistance is defined as the distance from mean during an event year using event year ANPP and long term average ANPP. Recovery is defined as the return rate to mean following an event using post perturbation year ANPP. Invariability is defined as long term mean ANPP divided by long term standard deviation. Precipitation events are identified from SPEI and are drought and wet events which are climatic anomalies and are naturally occurring. The disturbance is treated as discrete event years and recovery is evaluated in the year after a perturbation which matches a pulse framing. Data were collected at Kellogg Biological Station Long Term Ecological Research site in Hickory Corners Michigan with coordinates 42 degrees 24 minutes N and minus 85 degrees 22 minutes W. The first author affiliation is Indiana University Bloomington Indiana USA. The study system is early successional grassland communities and the measured subject is plant community productivity ANPP. The observation window is 2000 through 2020 which is 21 years or 7670 days using 365 days per year.

Framework: resilience = capacity to tolerate fire without shifting to an alternative ecosystem state
Justification: The introduction defines post-fire resilience as the capacity to tolerate fire without shifting to an alternative ecosystem state. The study quantified post-fire tree seedling establishment in stand-replacing patches of 11 wildfires and analysed how establishment rates varied with post-fire drought severity and distance to seed source. Seedling establishment was measured from 184 field plots by recording every post-fire tree seedling in subplots and standardizing to seedlings per hectare per year. The disturbance included stand-replacing wildfire and post-fire drought severity during years 0 to 3 post-fire. The approach was field sampling conducted from June to August 2013 in Glacier National Park and the Greater Yellowstone ecosystem. The response variable used in models was the total number of seedlings present at sampling divided by years since fire which is a seedling establishment rate. Because resilience is framed as avoiding a shift to an alternative ecosystem state and the analysis evaluates whether regeneration is sufficient to maintain forest rather than shift to non-forest the quantification aligns with a threshold concept. The focal measured variable is tree seedling establishment expressed as density per area per time. The level is population because seedling establishment is quantified for tree species and for all species combined as plot level densities.
